title: Population dynamics of surface-mediated autocatalytic processes
abstract: We investigate the population dynamics of surface-mediated autocatalytic processes, in which particles diffuse in a complex environment towards surface regions where they can be either killed or replicated. These opposite mechanisms compete with each other and lead to a sophisticated stochastic evolution of the population size. We provide a systematic analysis of the generating function of the population size. We also deduce its distribution, mean, variance and higher-order moments. For this purpose, we employ several equivalent descriptions of these quantities in terms of nonlinear integral equations and partial differential equations with nonlinear boundary conditions. We inspect the long-time behavior of the population dynamics in three regimes when the mean population size vanishes, reaches a steady-state level, or grows exponentially. A numerical solution of the underlying integral equations and independent Monte Carlo simulations support our theoretical predictions.
summary: This work analyzes how a particle population evolves when diffusion is coupled with surface reactions involving both loss (absorption) and multiplication (catalysis). By deriving the generating function and applying advanced spectral analysis, the authors precisely map out three distinct long-term dynamic regimes—decaying, steady, or exploding—based on the relative strengths of the catalytic versus absorbing rates.
main_problem: The study investigates the population dynamics of autocatalytic processes where particles diffuse in a complex environment, undergoing competing mechanisms of killing (absorption) and replication (branching) at specific surfaces.
main_result: The long-time behavior of the population size is characterized by distinct regimes—decaying, steady-state, or exponential growth—determined by comparing catalytic and absorption rates against spectral properties of the underlying diffusion operator.
method: The analysis employs systematic theoretical tools including generating functions, transformation into nonlinear integral/partial differential equations, and advanced spectral methods (Laplace transforms) to solve for asymptotic limits.
model_or_system: The model describes particle diffusion in a bounded domain $\Omega$ with boundaries partitioned into an absorbing region ($\Gamma_a$), a catalytic region ($\Gamma_c$), and a reflecting region ($\Gamma_r$). The process is stochastic, involving both spatial transport and population change via branching.
key_observables: Population size $N(t)$, generating function $G_s(t|x_0)$, mean $\langle N(t) angle$, variance, and the asymptotic limit of $G_s$.
important_parameters: Diffusion coefficient ($D$), catalytic rate ($q_c$), absorption rate ($q_a$), and geometric parameters defining the boundary regions.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a bounded domain with smooth boundaries, allowing for spectral decomposition of the underlying diffusion operator; approximations are used for short-time behavior and asymptotic limits.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate random realizations showing fluctuations across different catalytic rates ($q_c$), and plot the critical lines separating subcritical ($\lambda_{-0} > 0$), critical ($\lambda_{-0} = 0$), and supercritical ($\lambda_{-0} < 0$) regimes based on reaction rates.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the physical setup (diffusion with boundary reactions) to deriving the governing mathematical equations for the generating function. It then systematically analyzes the long-time asymptotic behavior by transforming these equations into spectral problems and solving them in different rate regimes.
why_interesting: While focused on classical statistical mechanics, the rigorous treatment of nonlinear boundary conditions and the classification of dynamic regimes (sub/critical/supercritical) using spectral theory provides powerful mathematical techniques applicable to open quantum systems or reaction-diffusion models where boundaries impose non-trivial constraints.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A schematic view of a conﬁning domain Ω, whose boundary ∂Ωis partitioned into three subsets: an absorbing region Γa (in red) that can destroy each arrived particle with an absorption rate qa via A →∅; an inert region Γr (in gray) that reﬂects particles back into Ω; and a catalytic region Γc that can replicate each arrived particle into two independent copies with a catalytic rate qc via A →2A. Each of three subsets can be composed of multiple disconnected pieces (e.g., Γa = Γ1 a ∪Γ2 a). Two simulated trajectories are shown in blue and magenta: the ﬁrst particle is released from a point x0 (blue dot) and diﬀuses until its binary branching (green dot) on Γc; after this branching...

FIG. 2. 1000 random realizations (gray curves) of the popu- lation dynamics in the circular annulus with R = 0.1, L = 1, D = 1, qa = ∞, uniform starting point in the bulk, and three values of qc: 3.91 (a), 4.34 (b), and 4.78 (c). Solid red line shows the empirical mean (obtained over 105 realizations, see Appendix B). In the last panel, the logarithmic scale of the vertical axis results is cutting zero values.

FIG. 3. Contour plot of the principal eigenvalue λ− 0 as a function of qc and qa for the circular annulus with R = 0.1 and L = 1. The vertical dashed line presents qcrit c from Eq. (F6), while thick magenta line shows the critical line qc = µ0(qa) that separates the subcritical regime (λ− 0 > 0, top left) and the supercritical regime (λ− 0 < 0, bottom right). The function µ0(qa) is given by Eq. (F1).

FIG. 4. The probability ¯Q0(t|R) = ¯G0(t|R) as a function of time t in the circular annulus with R = 0.1, L = 1, D = 1, qa = ∞. Three panels illustrate three regimes: (a) subcrit- ical (qc = 0.9 qcrit c ≈3.91); (b) critical (qc = qcrit c ≈4.34); and (c) supercritical (qc = 1.1 qcrit c ≈4.78), with qcrit c = 1/(R ln(L/R)). The starting point x0 is set on the catalytic region (inner circle). Solid line presents a numerical solu- tion of the integral equation (19) as described in Appendix G; crosses show an estimation from Monte Carlo simulations with 106 particles (see Appendix B); dashed line indicates the asymptotic relations (37, 39, 48) in subcritical, critical, and supercritical regimes,...

FIG. 5. Mean population size N1(t|◦) for the circular an- nulus with R = 0.1, L = 1, D = 1, qa = ∞, uniform start- ing point in the bulk. Three panels illustrate three regimes: (a) subcritical (qc = 0.9 qcrit c ≈3.91); (b) critical (qc = qcrit c ≈4.34); and (c) supercritical (qc = 1.1 qcrit c ≈4.78), with qcrit c = 1/(R ln(L/R)).
